cards.front: What is the definition of community acquired pneumonia?
cards.back: An infection of the low respiratory tract resulting in parenchymal lung inflammation, typically associated with acute symptoms including cough, sputum production, shortness of breath, and pleural chest pain, with new infiltrates visible on chest radiograph.

cards.front: What is the incidence of pneumonia in South Africa?
cards.back: Approximately 400,000 cases per 100,000 population, with peak incidence in those aged 25-64 years, driven by high HIV prevalence in this age group.

cards.front: What is the HIV prevalence in hospitalized pneumonia patients?
cards.back: Approximately 70% overall, with rates as high as 90% in patients aged 25-44 years.

cards.front: What are the case fatality rates for pneumonia?
cards.back: Overall approximately 5%, rising to 12% in hospitalized patients and exceeding 40% in those requiring intensive care support.

cards.front: List the major risk factors for community acquired pneumonia.
cards.back: Older age, comorbidities (COPD, asthma, cardiac disease), immunosuppression (diabetes, HIV), recent viral infection, substance abuse, poverty, overcrowding, and pollution.

cards.front: What is the most common cause of pneumonia globally?
cards.back: Pneumococcus (Streptococcus pneumoniae) is the most common identified organism. However, no pathogen is identified in approximately 40% of cases even after invasive investigation.

cards.front: How has pneumococcal vaccination affected pneumonia epidemiology?
cards.back: There has been a global decrease in pneumococcal disease proportion following vaccination introduction. South Africa introduced the vaccine into routine infant immunization in 2009.

cards.front: What is the key finding regarding tuberculosis in pneumonia in South Africa?
cards.back: TB was identified in approximately 40% of hospitalized pneumonia cases in Durban, outranking pneumococcus. This was true in both HIV-positive and HIV-negative populations.

cards.front: What are the typical clinical symptoms of pneumonia?
cards.back: Cough, sputum production, shortness of breath, and pleural chest pain. Signs may be localized to lungs or systemic.

cards.front: What is the significance of chest radiography in pneumonia diagnosis?
cards.back: Chest radiography is the gold standard. A normal radiograph can exclude pneumonia except in specific circumstances. Clinical symptoms and signs alone correctly identify pneumonia in only 50% of patients.

cards.front: What are the predominant radiological patterns of pneumonia?
cards.back: Lower infiltration and interstitial infiltrates. The air bronchogram is a key radiological feature indicating consolidation.

cards.front: In which situations may a normal chest radiograph fail to exclude pneumonia?
cards.back: Elderly patients (immunosenescence), immunocompromised hosts, hypovolemic patients, and early clinical-radiological dissociation (where repeat radiograph at 24-48 hours is recommended).

cards.front: What is the CURB-65 scoring system?
cards.back: A severity assessment tool that awards one point for each of five components, determining pneumonia severity and guiding appropriate site of care. Widely validated since 2003.

cards.front: What is the role of C-reactive protein (CRP) in pneumonia management?
cards.back: CRP does not improve diagnosis with clinical evaluation and radiograph, but can be used where radiographs are unavailable. CRP failure to halve by 3-4 days identifies treatment failure risk.

cards.front: What is the role of procalcitonin in pneumonia management?
cards.back: Makes no additional contribution to diagnosis. May support decisions not to initiate antibiotics or guide discontinuation at 90% reduction from baseline. Not recommended for routine clinical use.

cards.front: What is the yield of sputum culture in pneumonia diagnosis?
cards.back: Approximately 15% in pneumonia. Only a minority produce adequate samples. Recommended in patients with risk factors and in all hospitalized patients with CURB-65 score >= 2.

cards.front: What is the diagnostic yield of blood culture in pneumonia?
cards.back: As low as 5%, rarely impacting clinical decisions except for Staphylococcus aureus. Recommended in patients with CURB score >= 2 or prior antibiotic exposure, but not in outpatient settings.

cards.front: What is the significance of urine antigen testing for pneumococcal pneumonia?
cards.back: Identifies an additional 11% of pneumococcal cases. However, does not influence antibiotic choices because empiric regimens always include pneumococcal coverage.

cards.front: What are the limitations of molecular testing for viral pathogens?
cards.back: Positive results do not imply causality (positive in asymptomatic individuals). Few effective treatments exist for viral causes. Tests do not exclude polymicrobial infection.

cards.front: What is the role of Gene Xpert assay in TB diagnosis?
cards.back: Cartridge-based nucleic acid amplification test replacing sputum smear microscopy. Has excellent diagnostic characteristics, surpassed only by sputum culture. Particularly useful in pneumonia.

cards.front: What is the urine-based LAM test?
cards.back: Useful in HIV-positive patients unable to produce adequate sputum for TB diagnosis. Sensitivity increases with advancing immunosuppression, approaching 85% in patients with CD4 counts under 50 cells/µL.

cards.front: What are the main etiological agents of pneumonia in HIV-infected patients?
cards.back: TB and pneumococcal pneumonia comprise the majority. Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (PCP) is the third most common pathogen, unique to HIV hyperendemicity settings.

cards.front: What are the diagnostic challenges of Pneumocystis pneumonia (PCP)?
cards.back: Accurate diagnosis requires organism identification on bronchoalveolar lavage. Immunofluorescence identifies at most 60% of cases. PCR tests have high false positive rates.

cards.front: What is the role of microbiological investigations in pneumonia treatment?
cards.back: Small and perhaps negligible role for upfront investigations. Clinical rules for antibiotic escalation are sufficient for identifying treatment failure.

cards.front: What percentage of pneumonia cases have no identified pathogen?
cards.back: Approximately 40% of pneumonia cases remain without identified pathogens even after invasive investigation, highlighting importance of empiric antibiotic therapy.

cards.front: What is the global epidemiology of lung cancer?
cards.back: Lung cancer is the second most commonly diagnosed cancer worldwide and the leading cause of cancer death for both men and women. It accounts for 1.8 million deaths worldwide.

cards.front: What is the epidemiology of lung cancer in South Africa?
cards.back: Lung cancer is the third most common cancer in men and the ninth most common cancer in women in South Africa.

cards.front: At what stage is lung cancer frequently diagnosed?
cards.back: Lung cancer is frequently diagnosed in advanced stages of disease, which contributes to low five-year survival rates and poor prognosis.

cards.front: What is the National Lung Screening Trial?
cards.back: A study comparing low-dose CT and chest X-ray screening in high-risk individuals (55-74 years old, current or former smokers with 30-pack-year history). It showed a 20% decrease in lung cancer mortality with low-dose CT compared to chest X-ray.

cards.front: What is the most important risk factor for lung cancer?
cards.back: Cigarette smoking is the most important risk factor, associated with causation in up to 90% of cases. It is the number one modifiable risk factor for lung cancer.

cards.front: How many carcinogens are found in a single cigarette?
cards.back: There are more than 300 chemicals in one cigarette, 60 of which are known carcinogens. Examples include nitrosamines and polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons.

cards.front: How do carcinogens in cigarette smoke cause lung cancer?
cards.back: Carcinogens cause DNA damage and mutations within tumor suppressor genes, which interrupt DNA repair and cell cycle regulation mechanisms, leading to tumor formation.

cards.front: How does duration versus intensity of smoking affect lung cancer risk?
cards.back: For patients with similar smoking histories, longer duration of smoking puts them at greater risk of cancer compared with higher intensity of smoking.

cards.front: What is the relative lung cancer risk in current smokers?
cards.back: Current smokers have the highest risk. Persistent smokers have a 16-fold elevated lung cancer risk, which is further doubled in those who started smoking younger than 16 years of age.

cards.front: How does smoking cessation affect lung cancer risk?
cards.back: Smoking cessation lowers the risk of lung cancer. The relative risk remains high in the first 10 years after cessation and gradually declines to approximately 2-fold at 30 years after cessation.

cards.front: Do modern cigarette formulations reduce lung cancer risk?
cards.back: No. Modern cigarette formulations do not reduce the risk of developing lung cancer; they appear to increase the risk.

cards.front: What percentage of lung cancer cases occur in past smokers?
cards.back: Almost 50% of lung cancer cases develop in past smokers, explained by the persistence of elevated risk for many years after smoking cessation.

cards.front: How does cardiorespiratory fitness affect lung cancer risk in smokers?
cards.back: Strong cardiorespiratory fitness may help reduce lung cancer risk in cigarette smokers and former smokers. Higher fitness levels are associated with lower incidence of lung cancer and better survival if lung cancer develops.

cards.front: What is second-hand smoke and its lung cancer risk?
cards.back: Second-hand smoke is carcinogenic smoke inhaled passively by non-smokers. Urinary levels of carcinogens in non-smokers are 1-5% of those in active smokers. Approximately 25% of lung cancers in non-smokers are believed to be caused by second-hand smoke.

cards.front: What other risk factors contribute to lung cancer development?
cards.back: Age, male gender, occupational exposures, diet low in fresh fruit and vegetables and fiber, low physical activity, radon exposure, asbestos exposure, HIV infection, and exposure to beryllium, nickel, copper, chromium, and cadmium.

cards.front: Are there lung cancers in never-smokers?
cards.back: Yes. A minority of lung cancers develop in people who have never smoked. These are genetically distinct from smoking-related lung cancers and often have detectable genetic mutations amenable to targeted therapy. They have a better prognosis.

cards.front: What is the relative risk of lung cancer with family history?
cards.back: A family history of lung cancer is associated with approximately a 2-fold increased risk of lung cancer development.

cards.front: What is radon and its association with lung cancer?
cards.back: Radon is an inert gas produced from uranium decay, present in the environment. It is well-established as a risk factor in uranium miners. Approximately 2-3% of lung cancers annually are estimated to be caused by radon exposure. However, household radon exposure has not been clearly shown to cause lung cancer.

cards.front: What is asbestos exposure and its lung cancer risk?
cards.back: Asbestos exposure is strongly associated with lung cancer causation, increasing risk by up to 5 times. It is also associated with malignant pleural mesothelioma and pulmonary fibrosis.

cards.front: What is the synergistic risk of tobacco smoke and asbestos exposure?
cards.back: Tobacco smoke and asbestos exposure act synergistically. The risk of developing lung cancer for persons who smoke tobacco and have asbestos exposure history approaches 80-90 times that of control populations.

cards.front: What is the relationship between HIV infection and lung cancer?
cards.back: Persons with HIV infection have higher lung cancer risk than those without. In HIV-positive people, lung cancer is the most common and most fatal non-AIDS-associated malignancy. HIV infection appears to increase lung cancer risk independently by a factor of at least 2.5-fold.

cards.front: What histological type of lung cancer predominates in HIV-infected persons?
cards.back: The majority of lung cancers in HIV-infected persons are adenocarcinomas (non-small cell carcinomas).

cards.front: Did HAART change lung cancer incidence in HIV-infected persons?
cards.back: In most studies, the incidence and risk of lung cancer in HIV-infected persons did not change significantly with the introduction of highly active antiretroviral therapy (HAART).

cards.front: What is unique about lung cancer in HIV-infected patients compared to the general population?
cards.back: Lung cancer in HIV-infected persons develops almost exclusively in smokers, but HIV-infected patients with lung cancer are significantly younger and present with advanced disease and shorter median survival compared to the general population.

cards.front: What occupational exposures increase lung cancer risk?
cards.back: Exposures to beryllium, nickel, copper, chromium, and cadmium increase lung cancer risk.

cards.front: What personal lung diseases increase lung cancer risk?
cards.back: Personal history of lung disease that scars the lungs increases risk, including COPD, tuberculosis, and certain infections.

cards.front: What role do biomass fuels and cooking methods play in lung cancer risk?
cards.back: Pollutants from cooking and heating with biomass fuel, burning coal indoors, burning wood indoors, cooking on wood stoves, and frying foods at high temperatures increase lung cancer risk.

cards.front: What is the evidence for dietary protective factors in lung cancer?
cards.back: Dietary fiber and vegetables have been suggested as protective. However, trials of beta-carotene, vitamin E, and retinol palmitate supplementation in at-risk persons actually increased lung cancer incidence.

cards.front: What is the evidence for cannabis smoking and lung cancer risk?
cards.back: Studies are showing that long-term cannabis smoking can increase the risk of lung cancer, but evidence is not yet as robust as for tobacco.

cards.front: What are global trends in lung cancer incidence?
cards.back: Lung cancer incidence has followed smoking trends with a lag time of several decades. Incidence is declining in developed countries (USA, Canada, UK) but expected to increase in developing countries with increased smoking.

cards.front: What is the lung cancer trend in South Africa?
cards.back: Lung cancer incidence is on an upward trend in South Africa. Notably, lung cancer incidence among women continues to increase despite very low smoking rates.

cards.front: Why do Chinese women have higher lung cancer incidence than European women?
cards.back: Despite very low smoking rates, Chinese women have higher lung cancer incidence than European women, with higher incidence of genetic mutations causing lung cancer.

cards.front: How is lung cancer classified histologically?
cards.back: Lung cancer is divided into small cell lung cancer (SCLC) and non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC). NSCLC accounts for 85-90% and includes squamous cell carcinoma, adenocarcinoma, and large cell carcinoma.

cards.front: How was small cell lung cancer traditionally staged?
cards.back: Small cell lung cancer was traditionally staged as limited or extensive disease.

cards.front: What are the characteristics of small cell lung cancer?
cards.back: Small cell lung cancer: accounts for ~15% of all lung cancers; almost always in smokers; characterized by small round blue cells approximately twice the size of lymphocytes; usually centrally located; aggressive tumor that spreads quickly; often has distant metastases at diagnosis; more responsive to chemotherapy and radiotherapy but prognosis remains poor.

cards.front: What are the characteristics of non-small cell lung cancer?
cards.back: NSCLC: accounts for 85% of lung cancers; can be centrally or peripherally located; often peripherally located; can be fast or slow growing; staged using TNM staging; has improved survival with newer treatments.

cards.front: What percentage of lung cancers is adenocarcinoma?
cards.back: Adenocarcinoma accounts for 35-40% of non-small cell lung cancers. It is the most common type overall, most common in women, most common in non-smokers (though most patients are smokers), and usually peripheral.

cards.front: What is the diagnostic implication of adenocarcinoma in non-smokers?
cards.back: If a non-smoker is diagnosed with lung cancer, they almost certainly have adenocarcinoma.

cards.front: What percentage of non-small cell lung cancers is squamous cell carcinoma?
cards.back: Squamous cell carcinoma accounts for approximately 30% of non-small cell carcinomas.

cards.front: What are the characteristics of squamous cell carcinoma?
cards.back: Squamous cell carcinoma: strongly associated with smoking; used to be the most common histological subtype but overtaken by adenocarcinoma; most common to cavitate; carries poor prognosis; usually central; difficult to resect because it grows into airways and close to mediastinal vessels.

cards.front: What are large cell carcinomas?
cards.back: Large cell carcinomas are the least common type of non-small cell lung cancers. They are usually peripherally located and very large, typically 1-4 centimeters on diagnosis.

cards.front: How does lung cancer typically present early in disease?
cards.back: Often lung cancer presents with no symptoms in early stages. Patients are asymptomatic until disease is advanced. In approximately 10% of cases, lung cancer is diagnosed incidentally on chest X-ray.

cards.front: What is the stage distribution at initial lung cancer diagnosis?
cards.back: At initial diagnosis: 20% have localized disease, 25% have regional metastases to local structures and lymph nodes, and 55% (majority) have distant metastases.

cards.front: What is the most common symptom of lung cancer?
cards.back: Cough is the most common symptom. New onset or worsening cough in a smoker or former smoker should raise suspicion of lung cancer.

cards.front: What does hemoptysis indicate in lung cancer?
cards.back: Hemoptysis (coughing up blood) is concerning for lung cancer.

cards.front: How does lung cancer cause dyspnea?
cards.back: Dyspnea occurs due to: mass effect of tumor pressing on airways, inducing pleural effusion that causes lung collapse, and wheeze when tumor presses on airway lumen.

cards.front: What causes chest pain in lung cancer?
cards.back: Chest pain develops when tumor invades outside the lung parenchyma into the pleura, ribs, and chest wall, or when enlarged lymph nodes compress surrounding structures.

cards.front: What causes hoarseness in lung cancer?
cards.back: Hoarseness occurs when tumor compresses the recurrent laryngeal nerve around the trachea that innervates the vocal cords.

cards.front: What is superior vena cava syndrome?
cards.back: Superior vena cava syndrome presents with headache, swelling of face, arms, and neck. It occurs when great vessels entering or exiting the chest are compressed by tumor, causing venous obstruction, blood vessel distension, and fluid backup in the region drained by the superior vena cava.

cards.front: What is a Pancoast tumor and what does it cause?
cards.back: A Pancoast or superior sulcus tumor is located at the apex of the lung. It compresses the sympathetic plexus, causing Horner syndrome.

cards.front: What are the components of Horner syndrome?
cards.back: Horner syndrome is characterized by: meiosis (persistently constricted pupil), ptosis (drooping of eyelid), and anhidrosis (inability to sweat on the same side as tumor). It does not cause problems with vision.

cards.front: What can result from progression of Pancoast tumors?
cards.back: Progression can lead to brachial plexus involvement, causing arm and hand weakness.

cards.front: What are the most common sites of lung cancer metastases?
cards.back: The most common sites for lung cancer metastases are bones, liver, brain, and adrenal glands.

cards.front: How do bone metastases from lung cancer present?
cards.back: Bone metastases present as localized bone pain or non-traumatic fractures due to bone weakness. Most commonly affected bones are spine, ribs, and pelvis.

cards.front: How do liver metastases from lung cancer present?
cards.back: Liver metastases present with jaundice, weakness, and weight loss.

cards.front: How do brain metastases from lung cancer present?
cards.back: Brain metastases present with a wide range of features including confusion, headache, nausea, vomiting, personality changes, and seizures.

cards.front: What are paraneoplastic syndromes?
cards.back: Paraneoplastic syndromes are clinical syndromes arising not from direct tumor effect (mass, invasion, metastases) but from immune response to tumor or tumor production of substances like hormones or cytokines that cause electrolyte abnormalities or hormone syndromes. They occur in 10-20% of lung cancer patients.

cards.front: Which lung cancer type most commonly causes paraneoplastic syndromes?
cards.back: Most paraneoplastic syndromes are caused by small cell lung cancer, though they may also occur in non-small cell lung cancer.

cards.front: Which lung cancer type most commonly causes hypercalcemia?
cards.back: Hypercalcemia is most commonly found in squamous cell carcinoma, though it can be caused by bony metastases or tumor secretion of parathyroid hormone-related peptide or calcitriol (usually from small cell lung cancer).

cards.front: Which lung cancer types commonly cause clubbing and hypertrophic osteoarthropathy?
cards.back: Clubbing and hypertrophic pulmonary osteoarthropathy are caused more frequently by adenocarcinomas.

cards.front: Which lung cancer type commonly causes thrombosis of hypercoagulability?
cards.back: Thrombosis and hypercoagulability syndromes are caused more frequently by adenocarcinomas.

cards.front: What is SIADH in lung cancer?
cards.back: SIADH (Syndrome of Inappropriate Antidiuretic Hormone) is more common in small cell lung cancer but can also occur in non-small cell lung cancer.

cards.front: Which lung cancer type commonly causes Cushing syndrome?
cards.back: Cushing syndrome is more likely to occur in small cell lung cancer.

cards.front: What are the clinical manifestations of hypercalcemia in lung cancer?
cards.back: Clinical manifestations are often referred to as "stones, bones, groans, and psychiatric bones" referring to renal calculi, bone pain, abdominal pain, polyuria, and signs of altered mental status. Common symptoms include weakness, fatigue, nausea, vomiting, and confusion.

cards.front: What is Lambert-Eaton syndrome in lung cancer?
cards.back: Lambert-Eaton syndrome is caused by antibodies against small cell lung cancer that cross-react with voltage-gated calcium channels on motor neurons, damaging them and leading to proximal muscle weakness and other symptoms.

cards.front: What are the muscle symptoms of Lambert-Eaton syndrome?
cards.back: Weakness of proximal muscles (difficulty raising arms, standing from seated), intraocular muscles (diplopia, ptosis), and pharyngeal muscles (slurred speech, dysphagia).

cards.front: What are the autonomic symptoms of Lambert-Eaton syndrome?
cards.back: Autonomic dysfunction results in dry mouth, blurred vision, impotence, and dizziness.

cards.front: What is the prevalence of Lambert-Eaton syndrome in small cell lung cancer?
cards.back: Lambert-Eaton syndrome occurs in 1-3% of small cell lung cancers.

cards.front: What is syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone (SIADH) in lung cancer?
cards.back: A paraneoplastic syndrome seen in around 10% of patients with small cell lung cancer, presenting with hyponatremia symptoms and potentially cerebral edema.

cards.front: What causes Cushing syndrome in lung cancer and its clinical features?
cards.back: Caused by glucocorticoids from lung cancer or ectopic ACTH. Features include hypertension, hypoglycemia, moon facies, muscle weakness, purple striae, and buffalo hump.

cards.front: What is hypertrophic osteoarthropathy in lung cancer?
cards.back: Syndrome with clubbing and wrist bone swelling, featuring symmetrical painful arthropathy of distal joints, wrists, and ankles.

cards.front: What aspects of history should be taken in suspected lung cancer?
cards.back: Presenting complaint duration, alleviating/aggravating factors, associated symptoms, medical history, comorbidities, risk factors (asbestos, smoking), family history, and environmental exposures.

cards.front: What physical examination findings suggest lung cancer?
cards.back: Weight loss, finger clubbing (especially in smokers), dullness to percussion from tumor or collapse, tracheal deviation, and pleural effusion signs.

cards.front: What symptoms and signs indicate possible lung cancer?
cards.back: Cough, hemoptysis, shortness of breath, wheezing, pain, pleural effusion, superior vena cava obstruction (Pemberton sign), hoarseness, elevated hemidiaphragm on X-ray, dysphagia, and metastatic symptoms.

cards.front: What chest X-ray findings suggest lung cancer?
cards.back: Single or multiple masses, pleural effusion, lung collapse, hilar lymph node enlargement, and cavitating masses. Normal CXR does not rule out small tumors.

cards.front: How does CT compare to chest X-ray for lung cancer detection?
cards.back: CT has sensitivity around 85% and specificity around 100% versus chest X-ray. Small tumors missed on CXR should prompt high-resolution CT if clinical suspicion is high.

cards.front: What information does CT provide for lung cancer staging?
cards.back: Tumor size, separate nodules, lung collapse, obstructive pneumonia, invasion of adjacent structures, proximal tumor extent, and involvement sites for biopsy selection.

cards.front: What imaging modalities stage metastases in lung cancer?
cards.back: CT of chest/abdomen/pelvis is standard. PET-CT using fluorodeoxyglucose detects metabolic activity and mediastinal lymph node involvement. MRI brain assesses brain metastases.

cards.front: What methods obtain tissue diagnosis in lung cancer?
cards.back: Sputum cytology, pleural fluid cytology, bronchoscopy with biopsy, thoracoscopy, thoracotomy, mediastinoscopy, and percutaneous fine needle aspiration.

cards.front: What does TNM staging classify in lung cancer?
cards.back: T: tumor size, airway relationship, structure invasion, nodule multiplicity. N: lymph node location (N1 ipsilateral, N2 mediastinal, N3 higher). M: metastases presence (M1 = stage 4).

cards.front: How is small cell lung cancer classified and staged?
cards.back: Limited: single-sided disease, one radiation field. Extensive: bilateral, pleural effusion, or distant metastases. Limited 15-25% five-year survival; extensive 20% two-year survival.

cards.front: What are three main treatment modalities for lung cancer?
cards.back: Surgery, radiation therapy, and systemic therapies (chemotherapy, immunotherapy, targeted therapy). Choice depends on stage and patient fitness.

cards.front: What criteria determine surgical resection candidacy?
cards.back: Tumor completely resectable in one lung, tumor small enough for lobe or lung resection, limited spread to ipsilateral lymph nodes, and patient fit for surgery.

cards.front: How does radiotherapy work in lung cancer?
cards.back: Direct DNA damage; normal cells repair faster than rapidly dividing cancer cells. Used after surgery, palliative stage 3B/4, endobronchially, and for brain/bone metastases.

cards.front: What are chemotherapy applications in lung cancer?
cards.back: Shrinks tumor pre-surgery/radiation (neoadjuvant), kills residual cells post-surgery, primary treatment for unresectable tumors. Selected stage 1-3 NSCLC get chemotherapy after resection; stage 3B/4 get chemoradiation or chemotherapy alone.

cards.front: What are targeted therapies in advanced NSCLC?
cards.back: Drugs targeting specific mutations (EGFR, ALK inhibitors) to stop growth/spread. For stage 3/4 with nodal involvement. Prolong progression-free survival and improve response versus chemotherapy alone.

cards.front: How do immune checkpoint inhibitors work in lung cancer?
cards.back: Target overexpressed inhibitory receptors/ligands on cancer cells that evade immune detection. Used for advanced metastatic NSCLC resistant to chemotherapy or combined with chemotherapy.

cards.front: What factors influence lung cancer prognosis?
cards.back: Stage (most important), metastases presence/location, targetable mutations, performance status, sex (females better with NSCLC), weight loss, pre-existing lung disease, elevated LDH/ALP, hyponatremia. Overall five-year survival ~20%.

cards.front: What is the definition of optimal use of antibiotics?
cards.back: Cost-effective use of antimicrobial agents that maximizes clinical effect while minimizing drug-related toxicity and the development of antimicrobial resistance.

cards.front: What are the three forms of antimicrobial drug use?
cards.back: 1. Empirical therapy - based on clinical/laboratory information before culture results

cards.front: What is the difference between time-dependent and concentration-dependent antibiotics?
cards.back: Time-dependent antibiotics (e.g., penicillins, vancomycin, cephalosporins, lincosamides): effectiveness depends on how long drug levels stay above MIC. Concentration-dependent antibiotics (e.g., aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones, macrolides): effectiveness depends on peak serum concentration relative to MIC.
cards.source: Optimal Use Of Antibiotics - Antibiotic Stewardship - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015

cards.front: How should time-dependent antibiotics be dosed?
cards.back: Time-dependent antibiotics are given frequently (e.g., every 4-6 hours) to maintain drug levels above the MIC. The goal is to ensure adequate time-above-MIC coverage throughout the dosing interval.
cards.source: Optimal Use Of Antibiotics - Antibiotic Stewardship - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
cards.sourceFile: Optimal Use Of Antibiotics - Antibiotic Stewardship - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015.yml

cards.front: How should concentration-dependent antibiotics be dosed?
cards.back: Concentration-dependent antibiotics are given as high doses once or twice daily to maximize peak concentration. This maximizes the concentration-dependent kill effect, achieving therapeutic success with less frequent dosing.
cards.source: Optimal Use Of Antibiotics - Antibiotic Stewardship - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015

cards.front: What are the key steps in antibiotic selection?
cards.back: 1. Make accurate clinical diagnosis and identify infectious vs non-infectious cause 2. Determine likely organisms based on site and clinical presentation 3. Collect appropriate samples before starting therapy 4. Base selection on pre-graduation education, scientific evidence, company information, clinical experience, local epidemiology, and local laboratory data 5. Always adapt national guidelines to local epidemiology and resistance patterns

cards.front: What are the reasons for antibiotic failure after initiation?
cards.back: 1. Incorrect diagnosis (not actually an infection) 2. Wrong type of antibiotic agent 3. Inadequate dose 4. Incorrect frequency of administration 5. Poor drug distribution to infection site 6. Inadequate host nutrition 7. Use of inappropriate combination therapy 8. Emergence of resistance 9. Other factors (abscess formation, foreign body, immune system issues)

cards.front: What are the adverse effects and dangers of antimicrobial agents?
cards.back: 1. Direct toxicity: hypersensitivity, encephalitis, rash, fever, blood disorders 2. Effects on normal flora: superinfection (Candida, C. difficile) 3. Masking of severe infection (e.g., abscess) 4. Drug tolerability issues 5. Selection and proliferation of drug-resistant organisms

cards.front: What are the indications for antibiotic prophylaxis?
cards.back: Medical indications: recurrent fever, recurrent UTI. Surgical indications: clean surgery (especially if involving prosthetic devices), lower limb amputation, endocarditis prevention, cardiac surgery with underlying conditions, surgeries with prosthetic devices.
cards.source: Optimal Use Of Antibiotics - Antibiotic Stewardship - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015

cards.front: What are the criteria for choosing a prophylactic antibiotic?
cards.back: 1. Risk of infection is high 2. Probable organism is identified 3. Agent is likely to be active against the organism 4. Narrow spectrum preferred (not broad-spectrum) 5. Agent should NOT be one used for treatment 6. Must be appropriate for the specific indication

cards.front: What is antibiotic resistance and why is it a major problem?
cards.back: Antibiotic resistance: organisms develop the ability to survive in the presence of antibiotics that previously killed them. It emerged after penicillin discovery in 1940 and now affects up to 100% of certain organisms in some areas. Resistance leads to high morbidity and mortality, is a major public health crisis, and requires continuous monitoring and appropriate use strategies.

cards.front: What are the main resistance mechanisms in bacteria?
cards.back: 1. Enzyme production (beta-lactamase, AmpC, carbapenemase) 2. Altered target sites (penicillin-binding proteins) 3. Decreased permeability (gram-negative outer membrane) 4. Active efflux pumps 5. Biochemical modification of the antibiotic molecule

cards.front: What is the definition of antimicrobial stewardship?
cards.back: Prudent use of antibiotics to achieve the best patient outcomes while minimizing antibiotic selective pressure, reducing drug resistance, and avoiding excessively adverse or undesirable dose effects.
cards.source: Optimal Use Of Antibiotics - Antibiotic Stewardship - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
cards.sourceFile: Optimal Use Of Antibiotics - Antibiotic Stewardship - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015.yml

cards.front: What are the core components of antimicrobial stewardship?
cards.back: 1. Confirm infection vs non-infection 2. Collect appropriate samples before treatment 3. Initiate empirical therapy after specimen collection 4. Monitor time to administration (should be rapid) 5. Re-evaluate at 48-72 hours (D2-D3) based on culture and sensitivity results 6. De-escalate, escalate, or tailor therapy according to results 7. Assess clinical stability and adjust accordingly
cards.source: Optimal Use Of Antibiotics - Antibiotic Stewardship - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
cards.sourceFile: Optimal Use Of Antibiotics - Antibiotic Stewardship - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015.yml

cards.front: What additional factors are important in antimicrobial stewardship?
cards.back: 1. Appropriate dose and frequency 2. Correct route of administration 3. De-escalation from IV to oral when appropriate 4. Appropriate duration of therapy 5. Whether to use single agent vs combination therapy 6. Monitoring for adverse effects 7. Regular team-based audit and feedback

cards.front: What is the proper duration of prophylactic antibiotics?
cards.back: Prophylaxis should be given within 12 hours pre-operatively or 1 dose before surgery. Duration: typically one dose for most procedures. If dosing extends beyond 24 hours, it is considered therapeutic, not prophylactic (even if only 3-5 days). Prophylactic antibiotics must be kept short to prevent resistance emergence.

cards.front: What is the triangle approach to antibiotic optimization?
cards.back: Three interconnected factors: (1) Drug: dose, pharmacokinetics/pharmacodynamics, concentration-dependent vs time-dependent properties, toxicity, infection site penetration. (2) Organism: susceptibility, virulence factors, resistance mechanisms, biofilm production. (3) Host: age, organ function (renal, hepatic), body weight, comorbidities, immune status, source of infection.

cards.front: How does patient age affect antibiotic selection?
cards.back: Neonates: many antibiotics are contraindicated or have altered metabolism. Common organisms in neonates (Group B Streptococcus, Listeria, gram-negative bacilli) differ from older patients. Drug penetration and metabolism vary significantly. Special dosing considerations (e.g., reduced renal/hepatic function) are critical.

cards.front: What host factors must be considered when choosing antibiotics?
cards.back: 1. Age and metabolic capacity 2. Body weight 3. Renal function (affects clearance) 4. Hepatic function (affects metabolism) 5. Site of infection (drug penetration requirements) 6. Immune status 7. Comorbid diseases 8. Nutritional status 9. Previous source of infection history
cards.source: Optimal Use Of Antibiotics - Antibiotic Stewardship - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
cards.sourceFile: Optimal Use Of Antibiotics - Antibiotic Stewardship - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015.yml

cards.front: How should antibiotic therapy be adjusted based on culture and sensitivity results?
cards.back: After 48-72 hours (D2-D3), review culture and sensitivity results. If organism is sensitive to initial choice: continue the same antibiotic. If resistance is found: step up to a more potent agent. Always tailor therapy based on susceptibility patterns rather than empirical choice.

cards.front: When is it appropriate to switch from IV to oral antibiotics?
cards.back: Switch from IV to oral when: (1) patient is clinically improving, (2) patient is tolerating oral intake, (3) appropriate oral agent with good bioavailability is available, (4) infection site allows for oral drug penetration.

cards.front: What is the role of a team-based approach in antimicrobial stewardship?
cards.back: Stewardship cannot be performed by one person alone. It requires a multidisciplinary team including: clinicians, microbiologists, pharmacists, nurses, and laboratory personnel. The team should: audit antibiotic usage, analyze data, provide feedback, establish guidelines, implement strategies, monitor compliance, and ensure adherence to stewardship principles.

cards.front: What strategies should an antimicrobial stewardship program employ?
cards.back: 1. Pre-authorization and restriction of certain antibiotics 2. Education and training for all staff 3. Development of institutional guidelines and clinical pathways 4. Antimicrobial cycling or rotation strategies 5. Combination therapy indications 6. Regular audit and feedback loops 7. Monitoring of resistance patterns 8. Analysis of antibiotic usage patterns

cards.front: What are the differences in antibiotic coverage needs for gram-positive vs gram-negative organisms?
cards.back: Gram-positive organisms: covered by penicillins, cephalosporins, vancomycin, macrolides. Gram-negative organisms: require different agents (e.g., fluoroquinolones, aminoglycosides, certain beta-lactams). Anaerobic coverage: requires specific agents. Chart shows coverage by drug class for each category.

cards.front: How does antimicrobial stewardship relate to infection site?
cards.back: Different infection sites require different considerations: UTI with complicated vs uncomplicated status affects antibiotic choice. CSF infections require agents with good CNS penetration. Localized infections (abscess) may require drainage in addition to antibiotics. The infection site also determines drug penetration requirements and local epidemiology.

cards.front: What is meant by narrowing antibiotic coverage and when should it be done?
cards.back: Narrowing coverage means switching from broad-spectrum antibiotics to more specific agents once the causative organism and susceptibilities are known. This is done at 48-72 hours when culture results return. Narrowing reduces selective pressure for resistance and minimizes impact on normal flora.
cards.source: Optimal Use Of Antibiotics - Antibiotic Stewardship - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015

cards.front: Why should national guidelines be adapted to local epidemiology?
cards.back: National guidelines are based on epidemiological data that may not apply to every institution. Resistance patterns vary by region and facility. Using unadapted national guidelines may select for resistant organisms at your facility. Local surveillance data must guide modifications to guidelines to achieve optimal outcomes while managing local resistance patterns.

cards.front: What is the clinical evaluation pathway from presentation to appropriate therapy?
cards.back: 1. Patient presents with disease/symptoms 2. Ask: Is this really an infectious disease? 3. Make clinical diagnosis 4. Ask: Infectious or non-infectious problem? 5. Identify likely organisms for that site/diagnosis 6. Collect appropriate samples (blood, urine, CSF, etc) 7. Send specimens to laboratory 8. Start empirical therapy 9. Lab performs microscopy, gram stain, culture, organism ID, susceptibility testing 10. Report results at D2-D3 11. Adjust therapy based on results
cards.source: Optimal Use Of Antibiotics - Antibiotic Stewardship - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015

cards.front: How are antibiotic susceptibilities determined in the laboratory?
cards.back: Laboratory performs disk diffusion tests (Kirby-Bauer): antibiotic discs are placed on bacterial culture plates. After incubation, inhibition zones are measured. Zones are compared to standard guidelines to classify organisms as sensitive, intermediate, or resistant. This determines which antibiotics are effective against the specific organism.
cards.source: Optimal Use Of Antibiotics - Antibiotic Stewardship - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
cards.sourceFile: Optimal Use Of Antibiotics - Antibiotic Stewardship - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015.yml

cards.front: What does a green color indicate on an antibiotic susceptibility disc test?
cards.back: Green color (or larger inhibition zone) indicates the organism is sensitive to that antibiotic (susceptibility typically greater than 80 percent). Orange/intermediate color indicates partial susceptibility (around 80 percent susceptibility). Red/no zone indicates resistance (the antibiotic is not effective). Full sensitivity is not guaranteed even with new agents.

cards.front: What are the factors affecting choice of antibiotic in uncomplicated vs complicated UTI?
cards.back: Uncomplicated UTI: typically simple organisms (E. coli), shorter therapy, oral agents usually sufficient. Complicated UTI: may have polymicrobial infections, structural/functional urinary tract abnormalities, requires broader coverage and longer duration. Choice of agent and duration depends on complexity and patient factors.

cards.front: What is the relationship between antibiotic use and antibiotic resistance development?
cards.back: Every antibiotic use creates selective pressure favoring resistant organisms. Resistance emerges within populations when antibiotics eliminate susceptible bacteria, leaving resistant strains to proliferate. Overuse and misuse accelerate this process. Appropriate stewardship minimizes selective pressure and slows resistance emergence.
cards.source: Optimal Use Of Antibiotics - Antibiotic Stewardship - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
cards.sourceFile: Optimal Use Of Antibiotics - Antibiotic Stewardship - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015.yml

cards.front: What are the components of combination antibiotic therapy?
cards.back: Combination therapy should only be used with clear indications. Unnecessary combination kills all susceptible organisms and creates space for resistant organisms to emerge. Combination therapy is indicated for polymicrobial infections, enhanced coverage when organism is unknown, or specific synergistic effects. Single agent therapy should be used whenever possible.

cards.front: What is the bench-to-patient clinical pathway in antimicrobial stewardship?
cards.back: Bench (Laboratory): microscopy, gram stain, culture, organism identification, susceptibility testing. Patient (Clinic): Initial diagnosis and empirical therapy selection, Re-evaluation at D2-D3 with lab results, therapy adjustment based on susceptibility, clinical monitoring for response and adverse effects.

cards.front: What is bronchiectasis?
cards.back: Bronchiectasis is the abnormal, permanent dilatation of medium and small bronchial trees. It may be focal or diffuse, resulting from transmural inflammation and destruction of the bronchial walls.

cards.front: What is the pathology of bronchiectasis?
cards.back: Transmural inflammation and destruction predominantly affects the walls of medium-sized bronchi. This leads to: mucosal edema, replacement of cartilage/muscle/elastic tissue with fibrous tissue, mucus gland hyperplasia with impaired mucociliary clearance, recurrent bacterial infections, increased bronchial wall vascularity (neovascularization), and risk of hemoptysis.

cards.front: What are the main mechanisms leading to bronchiectasis development?
cards.back: Four main factors: 1) Repeated infective insults 2) Airway obstruction (foreign body or lymph node) 3) Reduced clearance of mucus/materials in airways 4) Defects in host immune defense system. These lead to bacterial toxins, immune response with mediators and oxygen free radicals, progressive destruction, airway colonization, and ongoing destructive changes.

cards.front: What are the main etiological categories of bronchiectasis?
cards.back: 1) Airway obstructive causes (innate: bronchomegaly, tracheobranchomegaly; acquired: foreign body aspiration, lymphadenopathy) 2) Defects in host defense (immunoglobulin deficiencies - IgA, IgG; HIV; chronic malnutrition) 3) Impaired drainage (cystic fibrosis, Kartagener syndrome) 4) Secondary to connective tissue disease, alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency, chronic childhood infections

cards.front: What is Kartagener syndrome and what is the classic triad?
cards.back: Kartagener syndrome is a ciliary dysfunction disorder. The classic triad consists of: 1) Situs inversus totalis (complete mirror-image reversal of thoracic and abdominal organs) 2) Chronic sinusitis 3) Bronchiectasis.

cards.front: Which etiologies of bronchiectasis are associated with TB?
cards.back: TB causes bronchiectasis through: 1) Necrotizing effect on lung parenchyma and airways 2) Airway obstruction by fibrosis or lymph node enlargement. Apical or posterior segment abscesses should raise suspicion for TB.

cards.front: What are the typical clinical features of bronchiectasis?
cards.back: 1) Productive cough with profuse, purulent, foul-smelling sputum 2) Hemoptysis (occurs in majority of patients, due to friable mucosa or hypertrophied bronchial arteries) 3) Dyspnea 4) Wheeze (from associated bronchoconstriction) 5) Clubbing on examination 6) Coarse crackles/crepitations (from surrounding fibrosis and airway secretions)

cards.front: What are the major complications of bronchiectasis?
cards.back: 1) Hemoptysis 2) Septicemia or septic shock 3) Amyloidosis 4) Lung abscess 5) Empyema 6) Cor pulmonale with heart failure

cards.front: What are the three main goals of diagnostic evaluation in bronchiectasis?
cards.back: 1) Radiographic confirmation of disease 2) Identify if there is a potentially treatable underlying cause 3) Functional assessment of the patient

cards.front: What baseline laboratory tests are performed in bronchiectasis?
cards.back: 1) Full blood count 2) Inflammatory markers (to rule out infection) 3) Sputum microscopy, culture and sensitivity (to exclude TB and identify bacterial pathogens) 4) TB culture and Gene Xpert 5) Immunoglobulins (to assess for immunodeficiency) 6) HIV testing 7) Autoimmune screens

cards.front: What is the classic chest X-ray finding in bronchiectasis?
cards.back: Honeycomb appearance, which can be unilateral or bilateral, representing dilated airways and bronchial wall thickening.

cards.front: What are the key CT findings in bronchiectasis?
cards.back: 1) Airway dilatation with lack of airway tapering toward the periphery 2) Bronchial wall thickening 3) Cystic bronchiectasis (cystic-like appearance at the end of bronchus) 4) Signet ring sign (bronchus larger than accompanying artery) 5) Tram-track sign (elongated, dilated airways appearing parallel)

cards.front: What do pulmonary function tests show in bronchiectasis?
cards.back: Obstructive pattern: reduced FEV1, reduced FEV1/FVC ratio, and usually normal forced vital capacity. In advanced disease with extensive lung destruction and fibrosis, a very low FVC may be present.

cards.front: When is an echocardiogram indicated in bronchiectasis?
cards.back: To assess for right heart involvement due to chronic hypoxemia, including evaluation for right heart dilatation and pulmonary hypertension.

cards.front: What are the main goals of treatment in bronchiectasis?
cards.back: 1) Treat infections early, especially during exacerbations 2) Improve airway clearance 3) Reduce inflammation 4) Treat any identifiable underlying cause

cards.front: How are antibiotic infections managed in bronchiectasis?
cards.back: Antibiotics are used only during acute exacerbations and should be: 1) Early identified 2) Treated aggressively 3) Directed by culture and sensitivity results when available 4) Empirically started with augmentin (amoxicillin-clavulanate) combined with azithromycin. Note: Pseudomonas infection is particularly concerning as it's associated with greater lung function deterioration.

cards.front: What are the mechanical and physiotherapy approaches to airway clearance in bronchiectasis?
cards.back: 1) Oscillatory devices to loosen mucus and aid drainage/expulsion 2) Physiotherapy training on effective drainage techniques 3) Use of mucolytics (though controversial) 4) Bronchodilators to improve obstruction and aid secretion clearance. These methods help facilitate appropriate sputum drainage.

cards.front: When is surgery indicated in bronchiectasis?
cards.back: 1) Localized disease with poor response to medical treatment 2) Bronchiectasis with approximate airway obstruction requiring relief 3) Massive hemoptysis (may require bronchial artery embolization) 4) Selected cases for disease control

cards.front: What general preventive measures are recommended for bronchiectasis?
cards.back: 1) Smoking cessation (if applicable) 2) Pneumococcal vaccination 3) Annual flu vaccine 4) COVID-19 vaccination 5) Supplemental oxygen therapy if hypoxemia is present

cards.front: What is cystic fibrosis and what is the genetic basis?
cards.back: Cystic fibrosis is an autosomal recessive disease caused by a mutation in the cystic fibrosis transmembrane regulator (CFTR) gene on chromosome 7. The mutation affects chloride channels, resulting in viscid thick secretions that promote bacterial binding, proliferation, and recurrent infections.

cards.front: What are the multisystem manifestations of cystic fibrosis?
cards.back: 1) Lungs: bronchiectasis 2) Upper respiratory: chronic sinusitis with nasal polyposis 3) Reproductive: male infertility (reduced sperm motility/immobility) 4) Pancreas (exocrine): pancreatic insufficiency with protein and fat malabsorption 5) Pancreas (endocrine): diabetes 6) Liver: cirrhosis

cards.front: What is a lung abscess?
cards.back: A lung abscess is an infection of the lung parenchyma consisting of one or more necrotic inflammatory cavities that contain fibrino-purulent exudates. It typically presents as a cavity with an air-fluid level on imaging.

cards.front: What are the main causes of lung abscess?
cards.back: 1) Aspiration pneumonia (highest risk): seizures, coma, neurological disease (pseudobulbar palsy, bulbar weakness) 2) Bronchial obstruction from malignancy 3) Septic emboli from infected endocarditis or prosthesis 4) Direct spread from subphrenic or hepatic abscess 5) Complications of pneumonia (Staph aureus, Klebsiella, Pseudomonas)

cards.front: What is the relationship between patient positioning and aspiration pneumonia location?
cards.back: Patient positioning determines the location of aspirated material: 1) Supine (face up) → superior segment of lower lobe 2) Prone or lateral position → posterior segment (abdomen). This is because gravity and airway anatomy direct aspirated contents to different lung areas based on position.

cards.front: What organisms should be looked for in lung abscess workup?
cards.back: 1) Oral anaerobes (due to aspiration risk) 2) Staph aureus 3) Streptococcus pneumoniae 4) Aerobic gram-negative organisms 5) Anaerobic gram-negative organisms 6) Tuberculosis (especially if apical location). Sputum analysis is critical for organism identification.

cards.front: What imaging findings suggest lung abscess?
cards.back: 1) Chest X-ray: cavity with air-fluid level 2) CT: multiple cavitary lesions with air-fluid levels. The presence of an air-fluid level is highly suggestive of lung abscess.

cards.front: What are the antibiotic principles in lung abscess treatment?
cards.back: Drugs are directed toward anaerobic coverage using: 1) Metronidazole 2) Clindamycin 3) Combinations of such agents 4) Carbapenems. Treatment should continue for appropriate duration until clinical improvement and imaging demonstrates resolution.

cards.front: What factors should be assessed when antibiotics fail in lung abscess?
cards.back: 1) Is the bacteriological diagnosis correct? 2) Was the drug and dosage correct? 3) Has the abscess further complicated (empyema, bronchopleural fistula)? 4) Is there surrounding atelectasis/poorly ventilated lobule? 5) Does the host have poor immunity? 6) Is the treatment duration too short?

cards.front: When is intervention (beyond oral antibiotics) needed for lung abscess?
cards.back: If not responding well to oral antibiotic treatment, the abscess requires: 1) Postural drainage 2) Bronchoscopic drainage 3) Supportive care with analgesia. General measures include pain relief and antibiotics.

cards.front: When is surgery indicated in lung abscess?
cards.back: Surgical indications include: 1) Massive hemoptysis (medical treatment refractory) 2) Very large cavity with thick walls 3) Complicated by malignancy 4) Complications like empyema 5) Recurrent chronic lung abscess 6) Remaining residual cavity after treatment completion

cards.front: What are the major complications of lung abscess?
cards.back: 1) Empyema (with or without bronchopleural fistula) 2) Spillover/rupture from large abscess 3) Systemic septicemia or spread to other sites 4) Development of brain or other metastatic abscesses 5) Severe hemorrhage/hemoptysis 6) Chronic sequelae: chronic bronchitis, localized emphysema, bronchiectasis 7) Amyloidosis

cards.front: What is empyema?
cards.back: Empyema is a purulent exudative pleural effusion secondary to pneumonia, lung abscess, or bronchiectasis. It represents bacterial invasion into the pleural space with accumulation of pus.

cards.front: What are the risk factors for empyema?
cards.back: 1) Advanced age 2) Recurrent pneumonias 3) Alcohol or drug abuse history 4) Diabetes 5) Poorly controlled GERD (gastroesophageal reflux disease) 6) Poor oral hygiene 7) Immunosuppression 8) Previous pleural intervention

cards.front: What are the clinical presentations of empyema?
cards.back: Patients present as ill, toxic-looking individuals with: 1) Fever 2) Parrotic chest pain 3) Increased sputum production 4) History of bronchiectasis, previous lung abscess, recurrent pneumonias, or pulmonary TB

cards.front: What are the physical examination findings in empyema?
cards.back: 1) Medial spine shift 2) Tracheal deviation to the contralateral side (away from empyema) 3) Stony dullness to percussion 4) Reduced breath sounds

cards.front: What are the three stages of empyema and their typical durations?
cards.back: 1) Exudative stage (perineumonic effusion): ~2 weeks - normal pH, normal glucose, negative culture, reversible if treated early 2) Fibropurulent stage: 1-6 weeks - bacterial invasion, leukocytosis, low glucose, low pH, lactic acid accumulation 3) Organizing stage: ~2 weeks - fibrin deposit, formation of inelastic membrane, plural thickening, fibrosis

cards.front: What characterizes the exudative stage of empyema?
cards.back: The exudative stage (perineumonic effusion) involves: 1) Bacterial invasion into lung parenchyma causing pneumonia 2) Inflammation leads to reduced visceral pleural membrane permeability 3) Leakage of interstitial fluid into pleural space 4) High level of endogenous fibrinolytic enzymes 5) Laboratory findings: normal pH, normal glucose, negative culture. Early recognition and treatment prevents progression to fibropurulent stage.

cards.front: What characterizes the fibropurulent stage of empyema?
cards.back: The fibropurulent stage involves: 1) Bacterial transcendence into pleural membrane 2) Progressive leukocytosis and thrombocyte accumulation at pleural surfaces 3) Progressive tendency toward loculation 4) Bacterial and neutrophil phagocytic activity using up glucose, producing lactate 5) LDH rises in the effusion 6) Release of polymorphs and polynuclear cells 7) Laboratory findings: low pH, low glucose. This stage is more difficult to treat than exudative stage.

cards.front: What characterizes the organizing stage of empyema?
cards.back: The organizing stage involves: 1) Fibroblasts grow into pleural space forming inelastic membrane 2) PDGF (platelet-derived growth factor) and TGF-beta (transforming growth factor beta) lead to pleural fibrosis 3) Plural thickening develops 4) Thickening inhibits bacterial penetration, preventing antibiotic effectiveness 5) Microbiome biofilm formation reduces antibiotic effectiveness 6) Collagen deposition reduces fibrinolysis effectiveness 7) Surgery becomes essential and inevitable at this stage

cards.front: What imaging findings are seen in empyema?
cards.back: Chest X-ray shows significant fluid accumulation or complete 'whiteout' of the affected lung with tracheal deviation toward the contralateral side.

cards.front: What are Light's criteria and how are they applied to empyema?
cards.back: Light's criteria identify empyema as an exudative effusion. Key findings in empyema: 1) Exudative fusion (elevated LDH, high protein) 2) Low glucose (characteristic finding) 3) Low pH (<7.2) - diagnostic of empyema 4) Predominant cell type: neutrophil-predominant (except in TB empyema which is lymphocyte-predominant)

cards.front: What does a lymphocyte-predominant empyema suggest?
cards.back: Lymphocyte-predominant empyema suggests TB empyema rather than bacterial empyema, which typically shows neutrophil predominance.

cards.front: What are the aims of empyema treatment?
cards.back: 1) Control the infection 2) Allow re-expansion of the lung

cards.front: What antibiotics are used for empyema?
cards.back: 1) Clindamycin (often given) 2) Combined with metronidazole or ceftriaxone with metronidazole 3) Treatment duration: prolonged (2-6 weeks) 4) Directed therapy if organism cultured

cards.front: Why is intercostal chest drain the treatment of choice for empyema?
cards.back: Intercostal drainage is the treatment modality of choice because pus accumulates in the pleural space. Drainage via normal pleural aspiration alone will not adequately drain the thick purulent fluid. Without proper drainage, the fluid will loculate, making treatment very difficult. If neglected, loculation occurs and becomes increasingly refractory to medical management.

cards.front: What is intrapleural thrombolytic therapy and when is it used?
cards.back: Intrapleural thrombolytic therapy is administered (via cardiothoracic consultation) to dissolve fibrin and prevent loculation and adhesions. It is used when empyema becomes complicated and standard drainage is insufficient.

cards.front: When is decortication indicated in empyema?
cards.back: Decortication is an option when calcification has occurred within the pleural space, making the fibrotic pleural membrane restrict lung expansion despite drainage.

cards.front: What are the late complications of empyema?
cards.back: 1) Destruction of lung tissue 2) Development of bronchopleural fistula 3) Pyopneumothorax (pus and air in pleural space) 4) Empyema necessitans (spontaneous entry into chest wall) 5) Rupture into abdominal cavity leading to overwhelming sepsis

atoms.atom: CURB-65 severity scoring system
atoms.definition:
5-component scoring system (1 point each):
C = Confusion (altered mental status)
U = Urea >7 mmol/L (renal function marker)
R = Respiratory rate ≥30/min
B = Blood pressure (SBP <90 or DBP ≤60)
Age ≥65 years
Score determines site of care and mortality risk
atoms.clinical_relevance: Validated severity predictor since 2003

atoms.atom: Pneumonia Severity Index (PSI) alternative
atoms.definition:
Alternative severity assessment tool incorporating:
- Demographics (age, sex)
- Comorbidities (COPD, asthma, cardiac disease)
- Vital signs (RR, BP, temperature)
- Laboratory values
- Imaging findings
atoms.clinical_relevance: Predicts mortality risk; guides admission decisions

atoms.atom: CAP risk factor stratification
atoms.definition:
Major risk factors for severe CAP:
- Older age (>65 years)
- Comorbidities: COPD, asthma, cardiac disease
- Immunosuppression: diabetes, HIV, malnutrition
- Recent viral infection (influenza, RSV)
- Substance abuse (alcohol, IV drugs)
- Poverty/overcrowding/pollution exposure
atoms.clinical_relevance: Risk assessment guides empiric antibiotic strategy

atoms.atom: CAP mortality stratification by clinical context
atoms.definition:
Overall ~5% mortality; varies by setting:
- Outpatient: lower mortality
- Hospitalized: ~12% mortality
- ICU-requiring: >40% mortality
Case fatality rate influenced by access to antibiotics, comorbidities
atoms.clinical_relevance: Mortality risk guides treatment intensity

atoms.atom: Chest radiography as gold standard for CAP diagnosis
atoms.definition:
CXR is the gold standard diagnostic modality:
- Typical patterns: lower infiltrates, interstitial infiltrates
- Key sign: air bronchogram (consolidation marker)
- Normal CXR can exclude pneumonia EXCEPT in specific settings:
  * Elderly (immunosenescence)
  * Immunocompromised hosts
  * Hypovolemic patients
  * Early clinical-radiological dissociation (repeat at 24-48h)
- Clinical exam alone correctly identifies pneumonia only 50% of cases
atoms.clinical_relevance: Essential diagnostic tool; normal CXR does not rule out CAP in vulnerable groups

atoms.atom: C-reactive protein (CRP) role in CAP management
atoms.definition:
CRP utility:
- Does NOT improve diagnosis when clinical eval + CXR available
- CRP <100 mg/L has strong negative predictive value for 30-day mortality
- CRP failure to halve by 3-4 days identifies treatment failure risk
- Use only where radiographs unavailable
atoms.clinical_relevance: Prognostic marker; not diagnostic; helps identify treatment failure

atoms.atom: Procalcitonin in pneumonia management
atoms.definition:
Procalcitonin (PCT) role:
- Makes NO additional contribution to diagnosis
- May support NOT initiating antibiotics decision
- May guide discontinuation at 90% reduction from baseline
- NOT recommended for routine clinical use
atoms.clinical_relevance: Limited role; not standard in CAP diagnosis/management

atoms.atom: Sputum culture diagnostic yield and indications
atoms.definition:
Sputum culture yield: ~15%
- Only minority produce adequate samples
- Recommended indications:
  * Patients with specific risk factors
  * All hospitalized patients with CURB-65 ≥2
- Limited clinical impact due to low yield
atoms.clinical_relevance: Low yield but helps identify resistant organisms in severe cases

atoms.atom: Blood culture diagnostic yield in pneumonia
atoms.definition:
Blood culture utility: as low as 5%
- Rarely impacts clinical decisions except for Staph aureus
- Recommended indications:
  * CURB score ≥2
  * Prior antibiotic exposure
  * NOT in outpatient settings
atoms.clinical_relevance: Very low yield; selective use in severe/hospitalized CAP

atoms.atom: Urine antigen testing for pneumococcal pneumonia
atoms.definition:
Urine antigen findings:
- Identifies additional 11% of pneumococcal cases
- Does NOT influence antibiotic choices
- Empiric regimens always include pneumococcal coverage
atoms.clinical_relevance: Epidemiological tool; no impact on treatment decisions

atoms.atom: Molecular testing for viral pathogens in pneumonia
atoms.definition:
Molecular viral testing limitations:
- Positive results do NOT imply causality
- Positive in asymptomatic individuals (lack specificity)
- Few effective treatments for viral causes
- Does NOT exclude polymicrobial infection
atoms.clinical_relevance: Limited clinical utility; does not change empiric antibiotic strategy

atoms.atom: TB as major cause of pneumonia in South Africa
atoms.definition:
South African epidemiology:
- TB identified in ~40% of hospitalized pneumonia cases (Durban data)
- TB outranks Streptococcus pneumoniae as leading cause
- Occurs in both HIV-positive AND HIV-negative populations
- 70% of hospitalized CAP patients are HIV-positive
- Peak age 25-44 years in HIV+ patients
atoms.clinical_relevance: TB must be considered in all pneumonia cases in high-burden settings

atoms.atom: Gene Xpert assay for TB diagnosis
atoms.definition:
Gene Xpert MTB/RIF:
- Cartridge-based nucleic acid amplification test
- Replaced sputum smear microscopy standard
- Excellent diagnostic characteristics
- Surpassed only by sputum culture in sensitivity
- Particularly useful in pneumonia workup
atoms.clinical_relevance: Rapid TB diagnosis; enables early TB treatment initiation

atoms.atom: Urine LAM test for TB in immunosuppressed
atoms.definition:
Urine-based LAM test:
- Useful in HIV-positive patients unable to produce sputum
- Sensitivity increases with advancing immunosuppression
- Approaches 85% sensitivity in CD4 <50 cells/µL
- Alternative diagnostic tool when sputum unavailable
atoms.clinical_relevance: Useful in advanced HIV with low CD4; enables diagnosis without sputum

atoms.atom: Major etiological agents in HIV-associated pneumonia
atoms.definition:
Top 3 pathogens in HIV pneumonia:
1. Mycobacterium tuberculosis
2. Streptococcus pneumoniae (pneumococcal pneumonia)
3. Pneumocystis jirovecii (PCP) - unique to hyperendemicity settings
- Different pathogen prevalence by CD4 count
- PCP more common in advanced immunosuppression
atoms.clinical_relevance: TB and PCP must be excluded in all HIV+ pneumonia cases

atoms.atom: Pneumocystis pneumonia (PCP) diagnostic challenges
atoms.definition:
PCP diagnostic challenges:
- Requires organism identification on bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL)
- Immunofluorescence identifies at most 60% of cases
- PCR tests have high false positive rates
- Diagnosis requires bronchoscopy in many cases
atoms.clinical_relevance: Difficult diagnosis; high index of suspicion needed in CD4 <200

atoms.card_source: Optimal Use Of Antibiotics - Antibiotic Stewardship - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
atoms.atom: Time-dependent vs concentration-dependent antibiotics
atoms.definition:
Pharmacodynamic classification:
Time-dependent (effectiveness depends on duration above MIC):
- Penicillins, cephalosporins, vancomycin, lincosamides
- Dosing: frequent intervals (every 4-6 hours)
- Goal: maintain drug levels above MIC throughout interval
Concentration-dependent (effectiveness depends on peak/MIC ratio):
- Aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones, macrolides
- Dosing: high dose once or twice daily
- Goal: maximize peak concentration for optimal kill effect
atoms.clinical_relevance: Dosing strategy must match antibiotic pharmacodynamics

atoms.card_source: Optimal Use Of Antibiotics - Antibiotic Stewardship - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
atoms.atom: Empiric vs targeted antibiotic therapy
atoms.definition:
Three antibiotic strategies:
1. Empirical therapy: antibiotics based on clinical/laboratory guess
   before culture identification and susceptibility results
2. Targeted therapy: antibiotics based on identified pathogen
   from culture and susceptibility testing (directed)
3. Prophylaxis: prevention of infections (e.g., pre-operative)
atoms.clinical_relevance: Empiric at presentation; targeted at 48-72 hours when cultures return

atoms.card_source: Optimal Use Of Antibiotics - Antibiotic Stewardship - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
atoms.atom: Indications for combination antibiotic therapy
atoms.definition:
Combination therapy rationale:
1. Increase spectrum of coverage
2. Achieve synergistic effect
3. Reduce resistance emergence likelihood
4. Reduce dosage of potentially toxic agents (e.g., aminoglycosides)
5. Treat polymicrobial infections
Preferred for: intra-abdominal infections, TB (resistance prevention),
endocarditis (synergy), severe infections needing broader coverage
atoms.clinical_relevance: Use combination judiciously; single agent preferred when adequate

atoms.card_source: Optimal Use Of Antibiotics - Antibiotic Stewardship - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
atoms.atom: Major bacterial antibiotic resistance mechanisms
atoms.definition:
Five main resistance mechanisms:
1. Enzyme production (β-lactamase, AmpC, carbapenemase)
2. Altered target sites (penicillin-binding proteins)
3. Decreased permeability (gram-negative outer membrane)
4. Active efflux pumps (drug removal)
5. Biochemical modification of antibiotic molecule
atoms.clinical_relevance: Understanding resistance guides empiric therapy for resistant pathogens

atoms.card_source: Optimal Use Of Antibiotics - Antibiotic Stewardship - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
atoms.atom: Antibiotic coverage by gram stain classification
atoms.definition:
Coverage patterns:
Gram-positive coverage:
- Penicillins, cephalosporins, vancomycin, macrolides
Gram-negative coverage:
- Fluoroquinolones, aminoglycosides, certain β-lactams
Anaerobic coverage:
- Specific agents required (β-lactam combinations, clindamycin, metronidazole)
atoms.clinical_relevance: Empiric choice must cover likely gram-stain pattern until culture results

atoms.card_source: Optimal Use Of Antibiotics - Antibiotic Stewardship - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
atoms.atom: Antimicrobial stewardship core principles
atoms.definition:
Definition: Prudent antibiotic use to achieve best outcomes while:
- Minimizing antibiotic selective pressure
- Reducing drug resistance development
- Avoiding excessive adverse effects
Core components:
1. Confirm infection vs non-infection
2. Collect appropriate samples before treatment
3. Initiate empirical therapy post-specimen collection
4. Monitor time-to-administration (rapid initiation)
5. Re-evaluate at 48-72 hours (D2-D3) with culture results
6. De-escalate, escalate, or tailor therapy
7. Assess clinical stability; adjust accordingly
atoms.clinical_relevance: Structured approach reduces resistance and improves outcomes

atoms.card_source: Optimal Use Of Antibiotics - Antibiotic Stewardship - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
atoms.atom: De-escalation protocol at 48-72 hours
atoms.definition:
D2-D3 culture and sensitivity review:
- If organism sensitive to initial choice: continue same agent
- If resistance found: step up to more potent agent
- If organism narrower spectrum: de-escalate to narrower agent
- Switch IV to oral when: clinically improving, tolerating PO intake,
  appropriate oral agent available, site allows oral drug penetration
atoms.clinical_relevance: Structured review reduces unnecessary broad-spectrum use

atoms.card_source: Optimal Use Of Antibiotics - Antibiotic Stewardship - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
atoms.atom: Clinical assessment of antibiotic treatment failure
atoms.definition:
Reasons for failure after initiation:
1. Incorrect diagnosis (not actually infection)
2. Wrong antibiotic agent
3. Inadequate dose
4. Incorrect frequency of administration
5. Poor drug distribution to infection site
6. Inadequate host nutrition
7. Inappropriate combination therapy
8. Emergence of resistance
9. Abscess formation, foreign body, immune system issues
atoms.clinical_relevance: Structured assessment guides therapy adjustment decisions

atoms.card_source: Optimal Use Of Antibiotics - Antibiotic Stewardship - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
atoms.atom: Adverse effects and complications of antimicrobial agents
atoms.definition:
Major adverse effect categories:
1. Direct toxicity: hypersensitivity, encephalitis, rash, fever, blood disorders
2. Effects on normal flora: superinfection (Candida, C. difficile)
3. Masking of severe infection (abscess masking)
4. Drug tolerability issues
5. Selection and proliferation of resistant organisms
atoms.clinical_relevance: Monitor for complications; balance benefits vs risks

atoms.atom: Bronchiectasis definition and pathophysiology
atoms.definition:
Definition: Abnormal, permanent dilatation of medium and small bronchial
trees (focal or diffuse) from transmural inflammation and destruction
Pathophysiology:
- Transmural inflammation and destruction of bronchial walls
- Mucosal edema and cartilage/muscle/elastic tissue replacement with fibrous tissue
- Mucus gland hyperplasia with impaired mucociliary clearance
- Recurrent bacterial infections → destructive cycle
- Increased bronchial wall vascularity (neovascularization)
- Hemoptysis risk from friable mucosa or hypertrophied bronchial arteries
atoms.clinical_relevance: Progressive disease; early recognition and treatment critical

atoms.atom: Cigarette smoking as primary lung cancer risk factor
atoms.definition:
Smoking epidemiology:
- Most important modifiable risk factor
- Associated with causation in up to 90% of cases
- Carcinogens in each cigarette: >300 chemicals, 60+ known carcinogens
- Risk factors: duration > intensity; younger start age > older start
- Current smokers: 16-fold elevated risk (32-fold if started <16 years)
- Persistent smokers: highest risk
- Post-smoking: risk declines gradually to ~2-fold at 30 years
atoms.clinical_relevance: Smoking history essential in all CAP/chronic cough evaluation

atoms.atom: Advanced-stage diagnosis and prognostic factors
atoms.definition:
Stage distribution at diagnosis:
- 20% localized (stage 1-2)
- 25% regional metastases (stage 3)
- 55% distant metastases (stage 4) - MAJORITY
Negative prognostic factors:
- Advanced stage (most important)
- Presence of metastases (especially brain, liver, bone, adrenal)
- Poor performance status
- Elevated LDH/ALP
- Hyponatremia (SIADH sign)
- Significant weight loss
- Pre-existing lung disease
- No targetable mutations
Overall 5-year survival: ~20%
atoms.clinical_relevance: Most patients present with advanced disease; affects treatment intensity

atoms.atom: TNM staging classification for NSCLC
atoms.definition:
TNM Components:
T (Tumor): size, airway relationship, structure invasion, nodule multiplicity
N (Lymph nodes):
- N0: no nodal involvement
- N1: ipsilateral hilar/peribronchial nodes
- N2: mediastinal/subcarinal nodes
- N3: contralateral or scalene/supraclavicular nodes
M (Metastases):
- M0: no metastases
- M1: distant metastases present (= stage 4)
atoms.clinical_relevance: Stage determines treatment: surgery vs chemoradiation vs supportive

scenarios.name: Community-Acquired Pneumonia Severity Assessment
scenarios.description:
Patient presents with cough, sputum production, dyspnea, and CXR infiltrates.
Apply CURB-65 scoring to determine severity and site of care.

scenarios.name: CAP Diagnostic Approach: CXR and Biomarkers
scenarios.description:
Symptomatic patient: fever, cough, dyspnea.
Integrate CXR findings with biomarker results to confirm pneumonia
and guide culture ordering.

scenarios.name: TB Detection in High-Burden Setting (South African CAP)
scenarios.description:
Patient in TB hyperendemic area presents with pneumonia.
TB must be excluded in all cases; Gene Xpert enables rapid diagnosis.
Special considerations for HIV-positive patients.

scenarios.name: Antibiotic Selection: Empiric vs Targeted
scenarios.description:
Patient with confirmed/probable CAP requires empiric antibiotics.
Choice of agent depends on risk factors, local resistance patterns,
and pharmacodynamic properties. De-escalation at 48-72h when cultures return.

scenarios.name: Antibiotic Resistance Pattern Recognition
scenarios.description:
Healthcare-associated or community-resistant pathogen detected.
Empiric regimen must account for resistance mechanisms
and may require combination therapy or escalation to carbapenems.

scenarios.name: Lung Abscess vs Empyema Differential Diagnosis
scenarios.description:
Suppurative lung disease with cavitary finding on imaging.
Differentiate between lung abscess (parenchymal) and empyema (pleural).
Treatment differs significantly (antibiotics vs drainage).

scenarios.name: Lung Cancer Risk Stratification and Screening
scenarios.description:
Patient with smoking history presents with chronic cough or incidental finding.
Assess lung cancer risk based on smoking pack-years and comorbidities.
Guide imaging strategy (CXR vs low-dose CT) based on risk.

scenarios.name: TNM Staging and Treatment Planning for NSCLC
scenarios.description:
Confirmed NSCLC diagnosis; imaging and staging studies completed.
TNM classification determines treatment modality: surgery vs chemoradiation vs supportive.
Metastatic disease (M1) generally receives systemic therapy or palliative care.
