guide.body:
# Pulmonology — Complete Study Guide

**Generated:** 2026-05-04

## Table of Contents

1. Community-Acquired Pneumonia
2. Community-Acquired Pneumonia
3. Lung Cancer
4. Lung Cancer
5. Optimal Use Of Antibiotics - Antibiotic Stewardship
6. Optimal Use Of Antibiotics - Antibiotic Stewardship
7. Suppurative Lung Syndromes

### Community-Acquired Pneumonia

Welcome to this presentation on community acquired pneumonia presented on behalf of the division
of pulmonology and critical care.
I will be discussing the epidemiology of community acquired pneumonia with a focus on
data available from local disease surveillance activities.
I will cover the diagnosis of pneumonia, addressing nuances in a resource constrained
high TB HIV burden setting.
I will talk briefly about the importance of severity scoring before moving on to existing
and novel diagnostic methods for rapidly and accurately diagnosing pneumonia.
I will discuss some important considerations which arise from the high prevalence of HIV
and TB in our local population.
The management of pneumonia has evolved over the years to an evidence-based empiric treatment
strategy based on the risk profile of the patient and the severity of the illness and
so I will address some of the factors which inform our treatment decision making.
And lastly, I will be covering the important progress that has been made in preventing
this leading curable cause of death in South Africa.
The most widely used definition of pneumonia describes the disease as an infection of the
low respiratory tract resulting in perincomer lung inflammation.
This is most commonly associated with symptoms of acute infection including a cough of recent
onset, sputum production, shortness of breath and protic chest pain.
The diagnosis also requires the presence of new infiltrates on a chest radiograph.
To differentiate the disease from nosocomium pneumonias, patients should have no risk factors
for healthcare-associated pneumonia and should not be hospitalized or reside in a
long-term care facility.
Influenza and pneumonia along with tuberculosis are amongst the top 10 causes of death in
South Africa and represent the leading causes of death from curable infectious diseases.
The incidence of pneumonia arising from sentinel surveillance data is exceedingly high at
about 400,000 population.
The peak incidence occurs in those aged 25 to 64 years of age likely being driven by the
burgeoning HIV epidemic in this age group.
Indeed, the HIV prevalence in hospitalized patients with pneumonia is estimated at 70%
and has been shown to be as high as 90% in those aged 25 to 44 years old.
The case fatality rate for pneumonia overall is around 5%.
While this figure rises to 12% in individuals requiring hospitalization and over 40% in
those requiring intensive care support.
The risk factors for pneumonia are older age, the presence of comorbidities including respiratory
conditions such as COPD and asthma, cardiac disease, cerebrovascular disease and immunosuppressing
conditions such as diabetes and HIV.
A recent viral respiratory tract infection often sets an individual up for subsequent
superimposed bacterial pneumonia and substance abuse including alcohol, smoking and illicit
substance use or contribute to risk for pneumonia.
In South Africa and in many parts of the developing world structural factors including
poverty, overcrowding and pollution contribute to the burden of pneumonia.
The etiology of pneumonia has been widely reported from most of the world and there has been
a substantial amount of agreement amongst the global data on the key pathogens.
The world over mixed viral and bacterial infections are common and the identification of a single
organism by routine laboratory methods may be misleading in directing therapy.
While pneumococcal pneumonia has predominated as the microorganism responsible for most
cases of pneumonia there has been a global decrease in the proportion of disease attributed
to pneumococcus following the introduction of pneumococcal vaccination.
In South Africa the vaccine was introduced into the routine infant immunization schedule
in 2009 and we are likely to see the cohort effect of this in the adult population in
the near future.
Pediotic outbreaks of influenza A and lesionella for example have punctuated the otherwise
consistent pattern of epidemiology of pneumonia in this part of the world.
A key finding in settings of endemic tuberculosis such as South Africa is that tuberculosis
can and does present as an acute pneumonia.
Globally pneumococcus is the commonest cause of pneumonia across all treatment settings
when an organism is found that is.
Importantly in 40% of the cases even after invasive investigation a pathogen is not identified.
Note here that viruses and atypical organisms occupy the remaining spots in the top 5 causes
of pneumonia globally and that TB does not feature on this list.
However, in a prospective observational study of pneumonia in hospitalised patients in
Durban who were investigated by expectorated sputum, induced sputum and bronchoscopy were
required the picture was quite different.
In this high TB HIV burden setting tuberculosis was identified in almost 40% of cases of pneumonia
outranking pneumococcus in this population.
This was true in patients with HIV but it was also true in the HIV negative population.
The diagnosis of pneumonia has remained a clinical one requiring symptoms and signs of low respiratory
tract infection as well as radiological evidence of new infiltrates on chest radiograph.
The most commonly reported symptoms include cough, sputum production, shortness of breath
and puritic chest pain while signs may be localised to the lung or maybe systemic.
The importance of radiographic evidence of infection is highlighted by the limitations
of using only symptoms and signs to establish the diagnosis.
Although cough was found to be the most common art patient to complain, pneumonia was established
as the diagnosis in only 5% of these individuals.
Indeed, when the symptom of cough in the absence of other respiratory symptoms is accompanied
by a normal chest radiograph, the diagnosis of pneumonia is accurately ruled out.
In a systematic review and meta-analysis of using symptoms and signs based diagnostic
algorithms for pneumonia, this approach correctly identified pneumonia as the cause of the clinical
findings in only 50% of patients.
An additional and potentially more useful finding however was that the absence of tachycardia,
tachycardia, fever or hypoxemia reliably excluded pneumonia in over 95% of patients.
Chest radiography therefore remains the gold standard for establishing the diagnosis of
pneumonia and indeed a normal chest radiograph is able to exclude the diagnosis of pneumonia
except in very specific circumstances.
The predominant radiological pattern seen are those demonstrated on this slide including
lower infiltration as well as alternatively interstitial infiltrates.
The air bronchogram remains a key radiological feature for the presence of consolidation
indicative of pneumonia.
So some of those circumstances in which the chest radiograph may be misleading or said
it in a different way in which a normal chest radiograph may fail to exclude pneumonia indicated
on this slide.
These include the elderly where immunosinescence dulls the inflammatory response in the lung
or in immunocompromised hosts for the same reason and in the setting of hypovolemia when
inflammatory excitation is restricted.
Although uncommon early clinical radiological dissociation may occur and in this instance
together with the previously mentioned cases a diagnosis of suspected pneumonia can be made
with a repeat chest radiograph performed in 24 to 48 hours.
Maintaining the diagnosis of pneumonia despite a normal chest radiograph in the absence of
a suitable explanation for the absence of infiltrates is associated with inaccurate diagnosis
and the misuse of antibiotics.
A number of assessment tools have been developed to assist clinicians in assessing the severity
of pneumonia.
One such scoring system is the CURB-65 score which was derived by the British thoracic
society as a tool for deciding the severity of illness and for guiding the most suitable
site of care.
This scoring system has been widely validated since its introduction in 2003 but has never
been validated completely in South Africa.
Nonetheless, it has been recommended by the South African thoracic society because of its
simplicity and because its accuracy is similar to that of more complicated scoring systems
such as the American thoracic society's pneumonia severity index.
This scoring system awards a single point to the presence of each of the components
and accurately predicts the mortality associated with each level of disease severity.
Equally importantly, it provides guidance on the most appropriate site of care based
on that severity score.
The role of biomarkers in the diagnosis of pneumonia has been investigated.
Ultimately, neither the CD active protein nor procalcitonin contribute to improving
the diagnosis of pneumonia.
When clinical evaluation, history and examination are accompanied by a chest radiograph suggestive
of pneumonia, neither the CD active protein nor the procalcitonin add additional diagnostic
benefit.
The CRP can be used in settings where chest radiographs are not available as an elevated
CRP offers similar positive predictive value as a radiograph.
Randomized controlled trial data supports the role of CRP in identifying patients at
risk of treatment failure or death, where using a CRP failure to halve by 3 to 4 days
as a critical threshold is useful for identifying such individuals.
Interestingly, using a composite of clinical features including respiratory rate, temperature,
heart rate and oxygen saturation, performs similarly well for identifying treatment
failure as the CRP, potentially then obviating the need for this test.
In fact, in favorable patients, failure to resolve fever has superior test characteristics
to the CRP in identifying patients at risk for mortality.
The CRP under 100 has strong negative predictive value for 30-day mortality.
It is outperformed in predicting survival by the curb score.
Similarly, the procalcitonin makes no additional contribution to the diagnosis of pneumonia
and has limited prognostic value in the setting of low respiratory tract infections.
Randomized controlled trials evaluating the role of procalcitonin in guiding treatment
decisions reveal that the procalcitonin can be used to support the decision not to initiate
antibiotics.
However, a similar decision can be reached when history, examination and chest radiography
are objectively appraised.
It would appear that clinicians feel less confident to withhold antibiotics based on
their own findings when compared to a laboratory test such as the procalcitoninin.
A different problem arises from trial data which supports the role of the procalcitonin
to guide antibiotic discontinuation, where it has been shown that discontinuing antibiotics
once the PCT has been reduced by 90% from its initial value is safe and is not associated
with an increased risk of treatment failure or mortality.
On the other hand, doing so significantly shortens the duration of exposure to antibiotics.
Despite the high quality evidence, including a Cochrane review, subsequent observational
studies have revealed that despite access to the procalcitonin result, clinicians do
not discontinue antibiotics at this threshold and therefore the benefit of reduced antibiotics
exposure and shortened hospitalizations are lost.
So if the procalcitonin is going to be used and it is found to be reduced by 90% from
its initial value while on treatment, the action to be taken which is the discontinuation
of antibiotics must be followed through on.
In addition, the cost-effectiveness of a PCT guided treatment strategy has not been assessed
and the test remains fairly expensive and therefore present guidelines do not recommend
the test for routine clinical use.
The need for microbiological investigations in pneumonia are still hotly debated and
it has become clearer that there is a small and perhaps negligible role for upfront initial
microbiological investigations in the diagnosis of pneumonia and only limited impact of such
investigations on treatment decisions because existing clinical rules for antibiotic escalation
are in fact sufficient for identifying treatment failure due to the spectrum of cover being
inadequate.
Nonetheless, microbiological investigations may allow for more timely escalation of antibiotic
cover in settings where clinical algorithms are not closely followed resulting in direct
benefit to patients.
In instances where antibiotic activity can be narrowed, society at large benefits from
this principle of antibiotics stewardship.
In addition, the surveillance of etiological agents of pneumonia may continue to help guide
our choices of empiric antibiotics at regional and national levels.
Sputum evaluation is bedeviled by the need for an adequate sputum sample.
Only a minority of patients in fact would produce an adequate sample and the overall
yield of sputum culture is reported at approximately 15% in pneumonia.
In patients with risk factors for staphylococcus aureus pneumonia or gram negative infection,
sputum evaluation is recommended as well as in all patients with a curb 65 score of greater
than equal to 2 and who are therefore hospitalized.
The yield of blood culture is as low as 5% and really impacts clinical decision making
except when staphylococcus aureus infection is a cause and where the positive culture
result might be available early enough to outperform clinical decision rules for guiding
antibiotic choices.
Nonetheless, our local guidelines ask that we consider blood culture in all patients
with a curb score of 2 or more or in individuals with prior exposure to antibiotics and furthermore
that the test not be performed in the outpatient setting.
A reasonable role then for blood culture evaluation may also be in individuals who
are failing treatment for pneumonia.
Other tests such as the urine antigen test for streptococcal pneumonia has been demonstrated
in recent meta-analyses to identify an additional 11% of patients with pneumococcal pneumonia.
However, this did not influence antibiotic choices or ultra-clinical outcomes.
This is unsurprising given that empatic antibiotic regimens for pneumonia always include agents
for pneumococcal cover.
Similarly, urine antigen testing for lesionella is of limited value as the organism is relatively
uncommon in patients with non-severe pneumonia and in all patients with severe pneumonia
they are likely to be appropriately covered by empritic choices.
There have been significant additions to the diagnostic armamentarium by molecular methods
for detecting organisms, although most of these gains have been made in detecting viruses.
The drawbacks are that a positive molecular test does not necessarily imply causality
as the test is positive in a significant proportion of asymptomatic individuals.
Moreover, apart from influenza and perhaps more recently SARS-CoV-2, there are no effective
treatment options for the other common viral causes of pneumonia.
Even when these tests are positive, they do not rule out polymicrobial infection and
the withholding of antibiotics in such instances may be detrimental.
Testing for viral pathogens of pneumonia is therefore not presently recommended except
in patients with severe pneumonia during the influenza season and when effective treatment
with also tamarva with other antiviral agents is available.
Tuberculosis is a leading cause of death from a curable infectious disease and remains a
national and global health challenge.
The intersection of TB and HIV in our setting has intensified the epidemic.
The disease has remained a public health emergency in this country for more than a decade and
our Department of Health recommends annual screening for TB in almost all South Africans.
Local data on the etiology of pneumonia and hospitalized patients reveals that up to 40
percent of these individuals have tuberculosis.
The gene expert assay, a cartridge-based nucleic acid amplification test, has replaced sputum
smear as the initial test for TB.
It has excellent diagnostic test characteristics and is outperformed only by the gold standard
of sputum culture, which is limited in the setting of pneumonia by the long turnaround
time.
- -
In patients with HIV who are unable to produce sputum or who cannot produce adequate quality
sputum, the urine-based lamb test has emerged as a useful diagnostic tool.
It has a sensitivity which increases with advancing immunosuppression and approaches
a sensitivity of 85 percent in patients with CD4 counts under 50.
In patients with HIV, TB and pneumococcal pneumonia comprise the majority of cat.
However, pneumocystis arises as an important pathogen.
It is the third most common etiological agent of pneumonia in HIV-infected patients in
our setting.
This is a unique consideration in settings of HIV hyperindimicity and is of particular
concern given the high mortality associated with the disease, even when optimal treatment
is applied.
The challenge we are faced with is that the accurate diagnosis of PCP requires identification
of the organism on a Bronco-LViola-Lavage specimen requiring a relatively invasive process
of sample acquisition in a setting where bronchoscopy may not be widely available and in a subset
of patients who may not be suitable candidates for such an invasive diagnostic procedure.
Existing laboratory methods including immunofluorescence performs poorly on expected sputum or induced
sputum, identifying at most 60 percent of cases.
PCR-based tests have improved sensitivity but suffer from a high false positive rate
because the genetic material of the organism may be found in patients without the disease.
While B2D glucant testing capable of identifying fungal cell work components may be useful
in the diagnosis of PCP, its role has not yet been terrified in the setting of HIV and
is therefore not presently recommended for routine clinical use.
Given the significant burden of PCP in our setting and our limited diagnostic capabi

-

### Community-Acquired Pneumonia

- -
lities,
the South African pneumonia guidelines recommends using the WHO case definition for the diagnosis
of PCP in individuals with HIV.
The diagnosis requires the presence of disneyone exertion or non-productive cough or tachypnea
and fever, together with bilateral infiltrates on a chest radiograph and the exclusion of
a bacterial pneumonia.
Several non-intricious illnesses may mimic a community acquired pneumonia and some of
these are indicated on the slide and include pulmonary edema, pulmonary embolism particularly
when pulmonary infarction accompanies this as a complication, pulmonary hemorrhage, chemical
pneumonitis including aspiration, lung cancer and acute exacerbations of interstitial lung
disease COPD or asthma.
The treatment of pneumonia has evolved over the last two decades with a growing evidence-based
supporting an empilic antibiotic treatment strategy.
The considerations for selecting initial antibiotics include several important evidence-based
factors.
The severity of illness using scoring systems such as curve and therefore the site of care
is the first consideration.
Implicit in such a risk assessment is ensuring that older patients are also distinctly recognized
as is their predisposition to developing atypical pneumonias.
The use of antibiotics in the preceding 90 days has been associated with infection by
both in atypical organisms as well as resistant organisms.
The presence of comorbidities including the challenge of HIV in our setting have been
demonstrated to increase the risk of death from pneumonia and each of these comorbidities
may be associated with a predisposition to a certain pathogen.
It seems reasonable that they would form part of one's considerations for selecting
initial antibiotics.
- -
Drug intolerance is another obvious reason to eliminate certain drug classes from your
armamentarium while the ever-increasing call for antibiotic stewardship should serve as
a basis for dissuading us from selecting drugs without due considerations for the risk of
hastening the loss of these drugs to resistance and for minimizing the risks of antibiotic
exposure in our patients.
Taken together, we recognize that younger patients without comorbidities and who have
not recently been exposed to antibiotics can safely be managed based on the severity
of their illness alone.
Ourohydosomoxicillin is recommended for the treatment of this subset of patients with
curb scores of 0 or 1.
A curb score of 2 or more in this subset of patients requires broadening of cover to
include resistant streptococcus and atypical organisms.
To achieve this, treatment with amoxicillin cavulanate or a second or third-generation
kepusporin and a macrolide is necessary.
Those older than the age of 65 years or patients who have comorbidities or who have
been exposed to antibiotics in the preceding 90 days are offered broader antibiotic cover
even with less severe disease, recognizing their predisposition to resistant organisms
and atypical organisms.
As outpatients, they are offered a amoxicillin cavulanate or an oral second-generation kepusporin
while as inpatients, they may be offered a third-generation kepusporin to include cover
for gram-negative intracocki and highly resistant streptococcus.
The use of macrolides in this instance has become commonplace in such older patients
with comorbidities because of their increased risk of atypical infection.
Severe pneumonia or the requirement of intensive care support are indeed hard indications
to include a macrolide in the treatment of pneumonia.
While respiratory fluoroquinolones are an attractive alternative as they may be used
- -
in all instances, its strong activity against tuberculosis should drive us to protect the
drug from resistance so that we may preserve it for the significant challenge of drug-resistant
TB.
What about empiric therapy for PCP and TB?
Despite the diagnostic difficulty encountered with PCP, it is recommended that empiric treatment
be offered to all HIV-infected patients who meet the clinical case definition of PCP.
In these patients, no further investigation for PCP may be necessary.
In patients who are at risk but who do not fulfill the WHO case definition for PCP, specific
testing is recommended up to and including bronchoscopy.
Infected treatment for TB, however, should be more limited, especially in the present
era of molecular diagnostic tools.
However, two instances for empiric TB treatment remain.
The first is that of Mollary tuberculosis where the diagnosis can be made on chest radiograph
alone.
And the second is the instance of septic shock occurring in at-risk HIV-infected patients
where the threshold of risk in benefit shifts in favor of early empiric therapy.
Empiric treatment for influenza is a concern during the South African influenza season,
which occurs between June and September each year.
During this period, we should suspect the diagnosis in patients with pneumonia who have
a preceding history of upper respiratory tract symptoms.
Empiric treatment during influenza season is recommended for patients with risk factors
including pregnancy, HIV, diabetes, chronic lung disease, neurological disease, and in
individuals of older age.
In all other patients where the clinical suspicion is high, a nasopharyngeal aspirate for influenza
PCR can be performed to guide the decision on whether to treat or not.
In recent years, the search for adjunctive therapies to improve treatment success has
- -
intensified.
Statins were one of the earliest candidate adjunctive agents investigated given their
theoretical benefits of immunomodulation, anti-inflammatory effect, and direct antimicrobial
effect.
There is now extensive evidence to suggest that patients with existing statin use at
the time of an incident episode of pneumonia do better than patients without a similar exposure
to statin.
Patients who have been previously treated with statins therefore should remain on their
statin during their hospitalization.
The question of whether statins should be used specifically for the treatment of pneumonia
is less well answered.
A single randomized controlled trial, including 34 patients from Spain, provided the only
high quality trial evidence for attempting to answer the question.
Sadly, the trial failed to demonstrate significant declines in the cytokine profile, time to
clinical stability, or mortality.
The trial was underpowered to answer the latter questions definitively, but nonetheless did
not provide any support for the use of statins in the treatment of pneumonia.
Quotico steroids are now uniformly applied in the treatment of septic shock, where its
role in attenuating the inflammatory response has been clearly demonstrated.
Although a theoretical benefit might accrue in the treatment of CAP by way of reducing
the incidence of acute respiratory distress syndrome, the difficulty has been establishing
consensus on which steroid to take and what dose to apply or what the candidate length
of treatment might be.
A recent meta-analysis identified 12 trials of corticosteroids in pneumonia, 6 trials
in severe pneumonia, and 6 in moderate community acquired pneumonia.
An apparent mortality benefit was found in patients who met the criteria for severe community
- -
acquired pneumonia, but not in those who did not.
A similar benefit in the reduction of the incidence of ARDS was attributed to corticosteroid
use.
However, the use of steroids is not without risk, and an increased risk of hypoglycemia
requiring treatment was seen in patients treated with corticosteroids.
More recently, a randomized controlled trial of hydrocortisone in severe community acquired
pneumonia helped provide high impact evidence for this question.
The study showed that steroids reduced the risk of intubation and death in patients admitted
to the ICU with severe community acquired pneumonia.
Corticortisone at 200 milligrams per day for 4 to 8 days and initiated within 24 hours
of diagnosis was sufficient to achieve this effect.
However, caution should be applied in individuals with immunosuppression including HIV who are
not well represented in this trial.
Overall, there appears to be benefits in the use of corticosteroids in the treatment of
severe pneumonia.
Although the risk of hypoglycemia remains an important concern.
Some caution should be applied in establishing time to clinical stability in patients treated
with steroid.
A steroid may help to treat the inflammation and resolve some of the symptoms and resolve
some of the signs related to inflammation, and these should therefore not be used in
the establishment of resolution of infection.
It may therefore be reasonable to use steroids in patients with severe pneumonia requiring
ICU admission perhaps in those with CRPs more than 150 milligrams per liter and then to
remember some of the restraint that must be applied in treating patients with influenza
or tuberculosis for whole steroid exposure might be detrimental.
In particular, we should be mindful that the evidence does not yet extend to patients
- -
with immunosuppression.
Early switching from intravenous to oral antibiotics is a central pillar of antibiotic
stewardship.
It reduces the cost of care, the incidence of complications arising from intravenous
cannula and promotes a reduction in the length of hospital stay.
The switch should be made once clinical stability is achieved and oral antibiotics should be
used until a total treatment duration of 5 to 7 days is reached unless specific indications
to do so otherwise are present.
The assessment of clinical stability is made when the patient is hemodynamically stable,
has achieved stable disability parameters, is a febrile and has a normal mental state.
Importantly, the patient should be able to tolerate oral medication.
Patients can be discharged after 24 hours of clinical stability usually after at least
5 days of antibiotic treatment.
When patients fail to respond to antibiotic therapy, one should consider several possible
explanations.
The following factors may have some bearing on responsiveness to empiric antibiotic treatment.
These include the age of the patient and the extent of the disease where older age and
more extensive disease may require longer duration of treatment.
The organism responsible for pneumonia in a particular instance might be resistant to
the antibiotic selected through an empiric choice.
Unusual pathogens requiring tailored antimicrobial selection might be present.
The condition of the underlying lung may require either alterations to the drug type,
the dosage or the treatment duration.
Complications of pneumonia might have arisen including embalema, necrotizing pneumonia
or lung abscesses.
Furthermore, several important non-infective diseases might present as pneumonia and these
- -
must be considered if patients fail to respond to antibiotics.
Acute complications from pneumonia should be considered in any patient who does not
improve on antibiotic therapy.
While parent mnemonic infusions occur in almost 40% of cases, these infusions are usually
small, exudative and respond to the treatment of pneumonia.
However, direct infection of the plural space may also occur and may result in either
a complicated plural effusion or an embalema.
Pluristenthesis in the persistent or large effusion should promptly be performed.
The findings of a low plural pH, low plural fluid glucose and elevated plural fluid LDH
reflects bacterial metabolic activity and supports the findings of a direct plural infection.
If microorganisms are identified either by Gram-Staino culture, the diagnosis can
be revised to amperema.
In both instances, treatment with an extended course of antibiotics, usually with an intercostal
drain in place, is warranted.
Infection in immunocompromised hosts or with organisms which result in an necrotizing
pneumonia should prompt a close examination for lung abscesses.
Which when present require protracted antibiotic therapy for 4-6 weeks.
If there is no microbiological guidance for antibiotic selection, both Klebsiella and
sensitive Staphylococcus aureus should be covered for the entire treatment duration.
Unlike chronic lung abscesses, these patients generally have favourable outcomes, while
the need for percutaneous or surgical drainage remains infrequently required.
It is now a growing recognition of the increased risk of cardiovascular events in patients
presenting with pneumonia, especially in older patients with risk factors for cardiovascular
disease.
These events may be an acute coronary syndrome, acute or decompensated cardiac failure or
arrhythmias.
- -
The pathogenesis of this interaction is unclear, but in the case of acute coronary
syndromes, there is likely a link with the increased platelet activation seen during
pneumonia.
It is now recommended that all patients with risk factors for cardiovascular disease or
who fail to respond to standard therapy be evaluated for a cardiovascular event.
The precipitous nature of pneumonia is misleading, as there may be significant implications to
long-term health.
In the lung, a single episode of pneumonia is sufficient to trigger the vicious cycle
of bronchiectasis and recurrent pneumonia.
More recently, the long-term mortality following an episode of pneumonia has become of great
interest.
A meta-analysis of long-term mortality in such patients demonstrates that between 30 and
50 percent of patients are dead five years after an episode of pneumonia.
This relationship has been difficult to elucidate as causal, because there is understandably
significant confounding by common risk factors for pneumonia and for death, including advancing
age and comorbid disease profiles.
There is, however, evidence of excess mortality in patients who suffer cardiovascular events
or acute kidney injuries during an acute episode of pneumonia.
Follow-up studies in pneumonia have demonstrated a persistent pro-inflammatory and pro-coagulant
state in such patients as much as one year after an incident episode of pneumonia.
An additional and interesting finding is that patients who fail to demonstrate features
of a robust inflammatory response during an episode of severe pneumonia are at greatest
risk of mortality.
These patients can be characterized as having severe pneumonia based on physiological characteristics
but who do not demonstrate pyrexia, choles or severe elevation in the ACRP.
It is postulated that this dissociation between disease severity and inflammatory response
- -
reflects underlying immunological dysregulation, which may herald poor long-term survival.
Perhaps the most successful strategy in our response to the challenge of community-acquired
pneumonia has been the introduction of pneumococcal vaccination, initially in our childhood
vaccination schedule and later in our adult vaccination guidelines.
Certainly the pneumococcal vaccine has been effective.
It reduces the risk of pneumococcal pneumonia by 45-75%, even in individuals with HIV, albeit
predominantly in those with CD4 counts above 200 cells per cubic millimeter.
It also reduces the risk of invasive pneumococcal disease by almost 75%.
In advanced HIV, there have been no head-to-head trials of PPSV23 and PCV13, but there is promising
observational data for sequential immunization in this population.
Although there have been no prospective studies demonstrating a mortality benefit in patients
with advanced HIV, that is those individuals with CD4 cell counts under 200 cells per
cubic millimeter, the demonstration of a favorable induced antibody response and a good safety
profile of the vaccines has supported the role of vaccination in this population.
The 23 valent polysaccharide vaccine has largely been superseded by the 13 valent conjugated
pneumococcal vaccine, which protects against the 13 most common pathogenic serotypes and
is capable of inducing a more robust and more durable antibody response.
Nonetheless, both vaccines are available in South Africa and the current recommendation
for PPSV23 is for all adults 65 years or older or in younger adults with co-morbid
disease who are current smokers who are asplenic or who are HIV infected.
PPSV requires a second booster vaccination five years after the initial vaccination in
these patients.
The 13 valent conjugate vaccine is indicated in all adults 50 years or older.
Younger adults with co-morbid disease who are current smokers who are asplenic or who are
HIV infected should also be vaccinated.
In those at greatest risk for invasive pneumococcal disease, it is now acceptable to provide
- -
sequential vaccination with PCV13 first followed by PPSV23 a year later.
The efficacy of influenza vaccination is less predictable as the vaccine changes each year
in response to the profile of circulating strains of influenza viruses.
The vaccine has demonstrated efficacy in reducing the incidence of severe disease, hospital
admissions and mortality, but the magnitude of effect understandably varies each year.
The vaccine is available from March each year in South Africa and is indicated in all pregnant
women, adults 65 years and older or in younger adults with co-morbid disease.
It is also indicated in all healthcare workers.
In summary, community acquired pneumonia is a significant cause of morbidity and mortality.
The diagnosis relies on clinical and radiological findings.
Severity scoring is essential and the curb 65 score is an easy to perform tool in most
settings.
The extent of investigation should be guided by the severity of illness.
To boculosis and PCP, I important considerations in HIV endemic settings.
Initial antibiotic selection is empiric but is guided by the risk profile of the patient
and the severity of the illness.
Early intravenous to oral antibiotic switching is an important goal of care.
Short-term and long-term complications of pneumonia should be addressed and vaccination
is a crucial preventative intervention for pneumonia.

## Study Strategy

### Foundation
1. **Read sequentially**: Start with Community-Acquired Pneumonia (epidemiology, diagnosis, management), then Lung Cancer (etiology, diagnosis, presentation), then Antibiotic Stewardship (drug selection), and finally Suppurative Lung Syndromes (bronchiectasis, abscess, empyema).
2. **Extract key definitions**: Note clinical definitions, diagnostic criteria, and evidence-based treatment protocols.
3. **Map relationships**: Understand how infections, risk factors, and comorbidities influence clinical presentation and management.

### Active Learning
1. **Review by topic**: Create summaries for pneumonia management, cancer screening and diagnosis, antibiotic selection principles, and suppurative conditions.
2. **Clinical reasoning**: For each condition, trace from epidemiology → risk factors → pathology → clinical presentation → diagnosis → management.
3. **Practice application**: Consider how concepts apply to patient scenarios with different comorbidities (HIV, TB, age, immune status).

### Mastery Checklist
- [ ] Define community-acquired pneumonia, risk factors, and epidemiology in South African context
- [ ] Explain diagnostic approaches and severity scoring (CURB)
- [ ] Know empiric antibiotic selection based on patient age, comorbidities, and antibiotic exposure
- [ ] Understand lung cancer etiology, screening, and clinical features
- [ ] Explain paraneoplastic syndromes and their pathophysiology
- [ ] Know antibiotic mechanisms, resistance patterns, and stewardship principles
- [ ] Define bronchiectasis, lung abscess, and empyema with their etiologies
- [ ] Understand management principles for suppurative lung conditions
- [ ] Apply knowledge to clinical scenarios with multiple comorbidities

### Key Topics Summary
- **Epidemiology & Risk**: HIV prevalence impact, smoking-related risk, healthcare burden
- **Diagnosis**: Chest radiography, severity assessment, organism identification
- **Treatment**: Evidence-based empiric therapy, de-escalation strategies, stewardship
- **Complications**: Metastatic disease, paraneoplastic syndromes, resistant infections
- **Prevention**: Vaccination, screening, smoking cessation
